The word "cocktail" first appeared in print in 1806, defined as spirits, sugar, water and bitters. That exact mix survives on menus today as the Old Fashioned.
"Punch" likely comes from the Hindi word panch, meaning five: the drink's five parts of spirit, sugar, lemon, water and spice sailed to Europe with East India Company crews.
The Gin & Tonic began as medicine. British officers in colonial India drank bitter quinine tonic against malaria and added their gin ration to make it bearable.
Tiki is a Hollywood invention: Don the Beachcomber opened in 1934, Trader Vic followed, and the two feuded for decades over who really created the Mai Tai.
The Moscow Mule's copper mug was a marketing pact from 1941: a struggling vodka importer, a ginger beer maker and a copper mug seller pooled their unsold stock.
The Negroni was born in Florence around 1919, when Count Camillo Negroni asked the bartender to stiffen his Americano with gin instead of soda water.
Ernest Hemingway drank his Daiquiris doubled and sugar-free at Havana's El Floridita. The bar still serves the "Papa Doble" in his honour.
The Bloody Mary was first stirred at Harry's New York Bar in Paris in the 1920s. New York's St. Regis hotel later adopted it and politely renamed it the Red Snapper.
Iced drinks were once a flex of empire: Frederic Tudor, Boston's "Ice King", shipped lake ice to the tropics in the 1830s and made cold cocktails fashionable.
New Orleans' Sazerac, first built on French cognac and Peychaud's bitters, is among the oldest named cocktails still poured today.
American Prohibition exported the cocktail: out-of-work bartenders sailed for London, Paris and Havana, taking the golden age of mixing with them.
Vermouth takes its name from Wermut, the German word for wormwood, the bittering herb in its earliest recipes.
Jägermeister means "hunt master". Its 56-botanical recipe dates to 1934, and the glowing stag on the label honours the patron saints of hunters.
Soju is the best-selling spirit on Earth by volume. In Korea, dropping a shot of it into cold beer makes somaek, the original soju bomb.
The Singapore Sling was created at the Raffles Hotel's Long Bar around 1915, where sweeping peanut shells onto the floor is still encouraged.
The Piña Colada is Puerto Rico's official national drink, and two San Juan bars have argued for decades over which of them blended the first one.
Irish Coffee was invented at Foynes flying-boat terminal in the 1940s to thaw frozen transatlantic passengers, then perfected at San Francisco's Buena Vista Cafe.
A "toast" is literal: spiced toasted bread was once floated in communal wine to season it, and the guest honoured by the custom "got the toast".
Happy hour started as US Navy slang for a ship's scheduled entertainment period in the 1910s. The drinking sense came ashore during Prohibition.
The Martini spent a century drying out: Victorian recipes used equal parts gin and sweet vermouth, plus a dash of bitters.
Angostura bitters' oversized label is said to be a sibling mix-up (one brother ordered bottles, the other labels) that customers loved too much to fix.
The sherry cobbler, all crushed ice, citrus and sherry, was the most fashionable drink of the 1800s and single-handedly popularised the drinking straw.
Cachaça is older than rum: Brazil was distilling sugarcane decades before the Caribbean got its first stills. The Caipirinha remains its great ambassador.
In Goa, feni distilled from cashew apples holds a geographical indication like Champagne's: only feni made in Goa may use the name.
The Espresso Martini was mixed in 1980s London by Dick Bradsell, reportedly for a model who asked for something to wake her up, then mess her up.
Sake is not a spirit: it is brewed from rice like beer. Korea's soju and Japan's shochu are the distilled cousins.
The Old Fashioned is a retronym: 1880s drinkers, tired of newfangled liqueurs and garnishes, ordered their whiskey cocktail made "the old-fashioned way".
The Margarita is Spanish for "daisy", and the tequila Daisy appears in print decades before any of the half-dozen origin legends bartenders still argue about.
"Toddy" is an Indian word: from the Hindi tari, the sap of the palm tree, tapped and fermented across South India for millennia. British sailors borrowed the name for their hot whisky cure.
India drinks more whisky than any other country on Earth. Roughly one in every two bottles of whisky sold worldwide is poured there.
Sura, a fermented drink from the Vedic period, appears in the Rigveda over three thousand years ago, making India home to some of the oldest recorded drinking culture anywhere.
Old Monk, the cult dark rum from Ghaziabad, built one of the world's best-selling rum brands since 1954 with essentially no advertising, only word of mouth.
Asia's oldest surviving distillery sits in the Himalayan town of Kasauli, running since the 1820s, and still uses its original Victorian copper stills.
Mahua, distilled from sweet mahua blossoms, has been made by India's Adivasi communities for centuries. Banned as "country liquor" under colonial rule, it is now being revived as a heritage spirit.
In the Eastern Himalayas, tongba is millet beer served in a bamboo mug and drunk through a bamboo straw, topped up with hot water as the evening goes on.
Indian single malts arrived late and conquered fast: Amrut launched in Glasgow in 2004 to prove a point, and Indian malts now regularly beat Scotch in blind tastings and world rankings.
Raj-era clubs measured whisky in pegs, a unit India still uses: a chota peg is a small one, a burra peg a large one, and the word likely marched in with the British army.
In Kerala, toddy tappers walk rope bridges between palm crowns twice a day. The morning sap drinks sweet as neera; by evening it has fermented into toddy on its own.
Handia, a rice beer fermented with herbal ranu tablets, has been brewed by Adivasi communities of eastern India for centuries and is offered to guests and gods alike.
The Indian Army still issues a rum ration: dark XXX rum has warmed high-altitude postings for decades, and Old Monk built its legend in those canteens.
Bhang, a paste of ground cannabis leaves stirred into thandai milk with almonds and rose, is Holi's traditional drink and is sold at government-licensed shops in cities like Varanasi.
Indian bureaucracy gave whisky made in India its official name: IMFL, short for Indian-Made Foreign Liquor. Foreign, made locally.
Nashik in Maharashtra is India's wine country: vineyards along the Godavari now supply most of the country's wine, barely three decades after the first modern winery opened.
India's craft gin wave rose out of Goa in the late 2010s, distilled with local botanicals like gondhoraj lime, Himalayan juniper and curry leaf, and Goan bars pour it with pride.
The Patiala peg, an extra-large pour of whisky, is named for Maharaja Bhupinder Singh of Patiala, whose hospitality was engineered to leave visiting cricket teams unfit for the next morning.
India Pale Ale is named for the voyage, not the birthplace: brewers hopped and strengthened their ale so it would survive the months-long sail from England to India.
By sheer volume, the best-selling whiskies on Earth are Indian: brands like McDowell's No.1, Officer's Choice and Imperial Blue each outsell the biggest Scotch labels many times over.
Most Indian whisky is distilled from molasses rather than grain, which is why the EU refuses to call it whisky at the border while a billion people call it whisky at the table.
Urrak, the light first distillation of Goan cashew feni, exists only from March to May and almost never leaves Goa. Locals drink it long, with lemonade and a slit chilli.
The cashew itself is an immigrant: the Portuguese brought it from Brazil to Goa in the 1500s, which makes feni a four-hundred-year-old fusion drink.
Kerala's toddy shops, the kallu shaapus, double as destination restaurants: the fiery kappa, karimeen and duck curries exist to keep pace with the toddy.
Arrack, from the Arabic araq meaning sweat, was southern India's everyday spirit for centuries until state bans in the 1990s pushed it underground and into memory.
Colonial Madras and Calcutta had licensed punch houses by the early 1700s, taverns named for the five-part Indian drink that English sailors could not stop ordering.
Gujarat has been dry since 1960, and Bihar, Mizoram and Nagaland followed. Visitors to Gujarat can still drink legally on a government-issued liquor permit.
Mumbai's old bars are called permit rooms because the Bombay Prohibition Act of 1949 technically requires drinkers to hold a permit, and the name simply never left.
Judima, the sticky-rice wine of Assam's Dimasa people, won a Geographical Indication tag in 2021, the first traditional brew of the Northeast to get one.
The village of Sekmai in Manipur is so famous for its distilled rice liquor that the drink simply carries the village's name: sekmai yu.
Across the Northeast, rice becomes zutho for the Nagas and apong for the Mising of Assam, each brewed with hereditary starter cakes passed down like heirlooms.
Rajasthan's royal houses distilled heritage liqueurs of saffron, dates, dry fruits and dozens of spices; recipes like kesar kasturi survived the palaces and are bottled again today.
In Coorg, homemade wine is a household art: coffee, betta greens, ginger and gooseberry all get fermented, and weddings are judged partly on the house pour.
Goa still makes port-style fortified wines, a habit left by four and a half centuries of Portuguese rule, and no Goan susegad lunch is complete without a small glass.
Terracotta stills unearthed in the Gandhara region suggest South Asia was distilling spirits some two thousand years ago, among the earliest evidence of distillation anywhere.
Amrut, India's first single malt, takes its name from amrit, the nectar of immortality churned from the ocean in Hindu legend. A drinkable pun with scripture behind it.
Bengaluru earned its pub-city name in the draught-beer boom of the 1980s and now hosts more microbreweries than any other Indian city.
Kanji, the deep-purple drink of black carrots, mustard seeds and water left to ferment in the winter sun, is North India's homemade answer to kombucha, brewed for Holi week.
Solkadhi, kokum steeped in coconut milk, is the Konkan coast's pink after-dinner settler, poured after fish curries the way other coasts pour digestifs.
Lion, brewed in the Himalayan foothills at Solan since the 1850s, is Asia's oldest surviving beer brand and still sells in Himachal Pradesh.
The palm civet is nicknamed the toddy cat because it raids the collection pots that toddy tappers tie under palm blossoms, and it has a taste for the fermented ones.
Sharab, the Hindustani word for liquor, shares its Arabic root with both syrup and sorbet: shariba, to drink. One verb, three very different evenings.
